title: 'The academic community as a whole appreciates it when data is openly available' - Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam
content: For some, full Open Science practices are already a reality, whilst others are still struggling to grasp the full scope of what it entails. In any case, it is a fact that VU Amsterdam prioritises Open Science. The new Guidelines clarify how this can be integrated into academic career paths. Co-authors Elco van Burg and Jacco Ossenbruggen explain.

title: Overview of Netherlands University Presses and their compliance with the Diamond Open Access Standard | Zenodo
content: The expertise center enouraged that Netherlands University Presses (NUPs) take the DOAS self-assessment developed by DIAMAS, and now part of the EDCH. The diamond open access standard (DOAS) is a benchmarking resource for institutional publishers and service providers (IPSPs) of diamond open access, providing 1) comprehensive guidelines to scholarly standards and 2) a self-assessment tool to evaluate publishing standards. The expertise center has made an overview of the key results of the self-assessment, including highlights of areas the NUPs perform well and areas for improvement. The main results show NUPs perform well and have scored high on most governance and policy aspects, especially in the components of Open Science and legal ownership. A number of items in two operational areas can be improved by these, mostly new presses based on results: 1) editorial management, editorial quality and research integrity and 2) visibility, communication, marketing, and impact.

title: Open Access journals published in Belgium without author fees – The Scholarly Tales
content:
A first version of the dataset dedicated to the landscape of Belgian Open Access (OA) journals which do not charge author fees has been published with DOI 10.5281/zenodo.20760254.

This overview, compiled by Laura Mesotten and Demmy Verbeke, provides a comprehensive list of Belgian Diamond OA journals – defined as journals that are published in OA without charging author fees and that are governed by the academic community. The dataset includes a range of bibliographical, editorial, and technical details, such as ISSNs, publishing software used, funding sources, and peer review status. In addition, the dataset documents the operational Diamond OA criteria for journals as developed in the context of the DIAMAS and CRAFT-OA projects. As such, it serves as a practical tool for identifying journals that are eligible for inclusion in the Diamond Discovery Hub maintained by the European Diamond Capacity Hub.

title: The Belgian Diamond Open Access Landscape | Zenodo
content:
This dataset of Belgian Diamond Open Access journals is produced and maintained by Laura Mesotten and Demmy Verbeke. It includes journals that meet the dataset’s defined criteria for both Diamond Open Access and Belgian (see the “About” section for terminology, scope, and methodology).

The dataset reflects the state of the data as reviewed at a specific point in time, indicated in the “About” section. As journal policies, platforms, and indexing statuses may change over time, the dataset should be interpreted as a snapshot rather than a definitive or exhaustive list.

While efforts were made to be as complete as possible (within the defined parameters), some journals may be missing, outdated, or require correction. Please contact the authors with any additions, corrections, or remarks.

The dataset is intended to support research and analysis on scholarly communication and Diamond Open Access publishing in Belgium. It will be periodically updated, and users are encouraged to consult the most recent version.

title: Open Access Scenario Analyses (OASA) - Open Access Scenario Analyses (OASA)
content:
"The Open Access Scenario Analyses (OASA) project addresses current challenges in the financial and organizational dimensions of the open access transformation through various analyses and provides recommendations for the continued development of this transformation in Germany. The goal of the OASA project is to analyze various financial pathways for open access development and to assess their potential impacts.

Through systematic examination based on scenarios, a comprehensive understanding of the opportunities, challenges, and risks of advancing the open access transformation is to be gained."

title: Goals and evaluations of transformative agreements: an exploratory study of consortial approaches | Insights
content: Abstract: Since the first transformative agreement (TA) in 2015, engagement in TAs has increased, as evidenced by the growing number of agreements in the Efficiencies Standards for Article Charges (ESAC) TA registry. This study aims to determine whether library consortia involved in TAs have clear goals and if they assess the agreements they negotiate. These two questions are answered by a literature review and an examination of websites and reports from 18 consortia with 15 or more TAs in the ESAC registry. The findings indicate that most consortia in the sample had a defined goal and had undertaken some form of assessment, although the depth and breadth of both varies widely. Some of the main themes that emerged were cost-neutrality, an increase in open access (OA) papers and a desire to pay for publishing rather than subscriptions. A key takeaway of this investigation was the importance of evaluating each agreement individually, due to the vast differences between TAs. An overarching theme was the role of research assessment practices, journal prestige and the perceived need for researchers to publish in certain publications – all factors that constrain libraries’ decisions to

title: How Interactive Workshops Kickstarted OER Creation During Summer Sprint | IU Libraries Blogs
content:
"Creating Open Educational Resources (OER) can feel like a daunting task. From understanding open licensing to navigating publishing platforms and designing accessible materials, the process often raises the same question: Where do I begin? This summer, the 2026 Summer Sprint for Zero Textbook Costs cohort demonstrated that one of the best ways to answer that question is by bringing people together to learn these foundational concepts.  

Rather than asking faculty to tackle OER creation independently, the Summer Sprint was designed as an interactive, collaborative experience that combined practical workshops with dedicated working time. Using a fast-paced approach, over a two-day period, participants learned the fundamentals of open education but also had opportunities to immediately apply what they learned to their own projects. Thus, kickstarting their projects!"

title: Open Access Journals in the Field of Chemistry: An Analytical Study of the DOAJ: Collection Management: Vol 0, No 0
content: Abstract: The main purpose of the study was to analyze various aspects of open-access journals in the field of chemistry listed in the Directory of Open Access Journals (DOAJ). The DOAJ is a platform that provides access to open-access journals that have undergone quality control processes. It serves as a comprehensive directory cataloging open-access scientific and scholarly journals across various subject categories. A survey methodology is well suited to investigating the evolution and current state of open-access journals in chemistry, following a comprehensive analysis. The study identified 315 open-access chemistry journals listed in the DOAJ, published in 16 languages by 101 publishers across 48 countries globally. General Chemistry (175) has the highest number of journals in the subdomain, followed by Organic & Bio-Chemistry (85). English is the predominant language of publication, with 295 journals exclusively using it. Switzerland ranks first with 51 publications in the DOAJ of Chemistry, followed by the United Kingdom with 46 journals. Most journals impose manuscript processing fees, while nearly one-third do not levy any charges. Moreover, nearly all journals use the

title: A decade of open access: Celebrating 10 years as a DOAJ Ambassador and Managing Editor for the MENA Region – DOAJ Blog
content: "It is hard to believe that a full decade has passed since I first stepped into my role with the Directory of Open Access Journals (DOAJ). Looking back at where we started, the landscape of scholarly publishing in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) region looked vastly different. Today, as I mark my 10th anniversary serving as both a DOAJ Ambassador and a Managing Editor, I am filled with immense pride for how far our regional research community has come and a sharp clarity about the work that still lies ahead."

title: WS#15 - Embrace the Open: What do we know about reusing OE content? - YouTube
content:
"What do we know about reusing OE content? Media, format, technologies and software, logos & metadata” is the fifteenth ENOEL Workshop of the "Embrace the Open" series, facilitated by David Stöllger (TIB, Germany) and Sylvia Moes (SURF/Npuls, The Netherlands). Our guest speaker was Harry Stokhof (HAN University of Applied Sciences, The Netherlands).

Learning outcomes You will have gained insights into the following questions:


	How can we improve the reuse of open educational resources?
	What does this require in terms of content creation?
	What is the role of quality (standards)?
	How can we make open educational resources easier to find for reuse?
	How can we work together on the issues we do not yet have answers to?"
snippet: "What do we know about reusing OE content? Media, format, technologies and software, logos & metadata” is the fifteenth ENOEL Workshop of the "Embrace the Open" series, facilitated by David Stöllger

title: Want to Make Science Work Better? Let’s Talk About How. | ICOR
content:
"In April, an Action Collaborative that emerged from the NASEM Roundtable for Aligning Incentives for Open Scholarship hosted its first in-person meeting at the HHMI Headquarters in Chevy Chase. The Action Collaborative focuses on designing a new research communication paradigm that rethinks how research outputs are shared, versioned, reviewed, verified, and synthesised. The group has been meeting monthly since August of 2025 and has accelerated progress during the in-person event. A result of that acceleration is a reshaping of the Action Collaborative to knit together work across many other groups and efforts, and to weave a larger message and roadmap for change. 

This ICOR meeting will focus on ‘Designing a new research communication paradigm,’ sharing the accomplishments so far, opening the floor for feedback and ideas, and seeking to broaden participation from communities working on open science, research integrity, and advancing faster, better outcomes."

title: Historic weather data made available to scientists and public, spanning Antarctica to Greenland | Royal Society
content: "An archive of over 1,600 sets of meteorological, magnetic and tidal observations taken from 1706-1915 from across the globe has been digitised for the first time. The treasure trove of historic weather data and imagery is now available for scientists across the globe to study on the Royal Society’s Science in the Making portal. Tens of thousands of individual observations show raw scientific data and weather readings from across all six continents and all oceans, from the Northern shores of Greenland to the Southern ice of Antarctica; tidal observations at London Docks to temperatures in London, Ontario; rainfall in Colombia to wind direction in Colombo, Sri Lanka. Highlights include a two-meter-long graph showing hourly magnetic observations in Singapore from 1844-45, a watercolour graph of weather readings from a regiment hospital in Karachi, Pakistan, 1858, and a dodecagon graph showing daily weather readings at Port Arthur, Australia, in 1840. Observations made in Antarctica during the famous Discovery expedition, led by Robert Falcon Scott and Ernest Shackleton from 1901-1904, can be explored through the handwritten notes and illustrations of Tasmanian physicist and

title: Illegal knowledge, illegal librarianship: On the intersection of black open access and the librarian’s professional ethics | LUP Student Papers
content: Abstract: The academic librarian’s mission of facilitating free access to knowledge has endured even as forms of librarianship have evolved. Since the introduction of the internet and the subsequent shift from physical to digital knowledge dissemination, additional ethical and practical considerations have emerged. One such consideration is the existence of black open access and shadow libraries, born out of a frustration with the oligarchic academic publishing industry’s treatment of knowledge as a resource to be profited from rather than a public good to be spread freely. This thesis conducts a rapid scoping review to examine the conflict between black open access and the academic publishing industry and attempt to position the librarian in relation to this struggle, both ideologically and practically. Black open access will be shown as an alternative mode of knowledge dissemination which support libraries in their work to preserve and spread knowledge, especially in times of crisis when libraries’ ability to fulfil those duties are compromised. Further, black open access will be evaluated both as a competition to academic libraries, but also as a force which we may utilise to

title: Republicans Are Dismantling a Key Tool in the War Against Kleptocrats | The New Republic
content: "Efforts to bring the barest transparency to U.S. shell companies stretch back to at least 2008. But it wasn’t until the early 2020s that legislators finally passed something called the Corporate Transparency Act. The bill was hardly partisan; remarkably, a slate of legislators from both sides of the aisle passed the bill over President Trump’s veto. Nor was the bill onerous. Instead of a public registry of corporate owners, as seen in places like the United Kingdom, America’s new shell company database would remain private, accessible only to federal authorities and other officials tracking illicit and looted wealth. It’s difficult to overstate just how momentous this new legislation was. For the first time in decades, the U.S. was no longer the leading font of anonymous shell companies. The best days of U.S. offshoring appeared behind us.... Barely a month into his second term, Trump announced that the Corporate Transparency Act was an “absolute disaster,” an “economic menace” that would “soon be no more.” He announced that his administration would no longer be enforcing the law for U.S. shell companies—and that no one would need to worry about prosecution for breaking the law. A

title: A comparison of preprint search aggregators: comprehensive identification of preprints in the information retrieval stage of evidence syntheses | Research Synthesis Methods | Cambridge Core
content: Abstract: This study investigated information retrieval of preprint records in the context of evidence synthesis work and compared 12 sources used to discover preprints. Identification of grey literature is often required or recommended in evidence synthesis guidance, and preprints are categorized as grey literature. The purpose of this work is to inform how and where to search for preprints to maximize coverage (through exploration of preprint server across aggregators and databases) while balancing search efficiency. Authors selected aggregators and databases hosting two or more preprint servers and then tested search functionality and extracted characteristics and features. Authors analyzed and compared the selected sources, tabulated the number of essential features, and created comparison tables reflecting database and aggregator features. The study protocol was registered in Open Science Framework registries. Preprint aggregators and databases differ in their content coverage, and their ability to design a comprehensive and reproducible search strategy. Limitations such as character or word limits for queries, limited advanced search operators, and missing export

title: Knowledge and Politics: Reflections on Challenges to Open Science
content: "This comment focuses on the contradictions inherent in current conceptualizations and practices associated with Open Science and highlighted by the preceding set of articles. Using Longino’s Critical Contextual Empiricism (CCE), it argues that Open Science offers the potential for realization of the norms envisioned in CCE, but that the economic forces capitalizing on aspects of the call for Open Science and the current global context erode the capacity to realize the fourth norm of equality of intellectual authority. This is a matter of both justice and epistemic reliability."

title: FAIR in practice: minimum metadata schema for bioinformatics analytics by machines | Journal of Biomedical Semantics | Springer Nature Link
content:
Abstract:  Background

One pillar of FAIR principles adoption is Reusability by machines to enable for example more efficient data analytics in fields such as Bioinformatics. However, it is not clear to what extent current metadata exposed by datasets and tools in common repositories enable this. In practice, metadata often lacks in machine actionability due to incomplete standardised metadata and lack of ontological descriptions.

Results

In this work, we identified minimal metadata that is needed to improve the machine actionability of bioinformatics tools and proposed a schema to address current limitations. The schema consists of metadata properties for the identification, selection, validation, and execution of tools. We also aligned this metadata to the metadata of datasets, in order to improve their integration for analytics by machines.

Conclusions

The identified minimal metadata improves the machine actionability of tools and data, and can be incorporated into platforms for tool and data sharing, and in FAIR infrastructures.

title: Postcolonial literature and the open access: A critical analysis | Journal of Social Review and Development
content: Abstract: The aim of this paper is to look at the questioning of the processes through which knowledge is produced, controlled, and disseminated via postcolonial discourse in the context of literature. This topic is relevant to the field of Library and Information Science (LIS) because of the current discussions about the dichotomy between open access (OA) and paid access to journals. While OA is considered more democratic, it is nevertheless built on previous systems of epistemic control. Drawing on novels such as ‘Nervous Conditions’ by Tsitsi Dangarembga, ‘Things Fall Apart’ by Chinua Achebe, ‘Purple Hibiscus’ by Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie, and ‘Weep Not, Child’ by Ngũgĩ wa Thiong’o, the study explores how access to knowledge is portrayed as a contested and politicized space. These texts reveal layered hierarchies formed through colonial authority, internal social stratifications, and acts of resistance. The analysis is carried out against the backdrop of the history of academic publishing starting from colonial missions' presses through the emergence of print capitalism to the development of OA. Despite calls for full-text accessibility through initiatives such as the Budapest

title: Developing Real-Time Research Data Visualization Dashboard for Academic Institutions and Libraries: Utilizing OpenAlex Database and Open-Source Software
content: Abstract: The case study proposed a solution for real-time research data visualization for academic Institutions. It developed a model of an interactive, real-time research datavisualization dashboard for academic institutions, using the OpenAlex database API and open-source tools such as Python, Flask, and Plotly. It compares OpenAlex and Scopus to assess the coverage of open-access databases and their future impact, highlighting API-based features for data visualization. Flask is utilized to build a dashboard (Web application) with API URLs, while HTML and CSS organize the charts, tables, colors, etc., on the front end for data visualization. The publication data of the top 10 QS-ranked universities were analyzed using both Scopus and OpenAlex to evaluate OpenAlex's potential as a reliable data source for research assessment and for the present study. The tools and the framework designed offer significant advantages to information professionals and libraries in creating an interactive, real-time research data visualization dashboard. Using publication counts at the institutional level as the unit of analysis, the Pearson correlation coefficient showed that OpenAlex coverage is

title: Who Will Keep Research Data Infrastructure Open and Running?
content:
"The global research enterprise relies on information infrastructure to power scientific discovery, medical breakthroughs, and evidence-based policymaking. But the data repositories, digital asset management services, and preservation systems that ensure research data remains open and accessible are often overlooked—until they disappear. Many of these tools and services are vulnerable to policy changes and funding cuts. Over the last 25 years, nearly 200 research data repositories have shut down permanently; more than half of those closures have happened since 2018.

Each closure represents lost knowledge and leads to broken links, bad citations, and a general inability to utilize and verify scientific findings. For example, without funding from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, the Alaska Earthquake Center has ceased providing real-time seismic data to inform tsunami warnings for the whole US West Coast. On topics as disparate as Gulf War illness or natural selection, when a repository goes dark, it can affect individuals or even entire research disciplines."

title: Peer Community In and Peer Community Journal: a two-step diamond OA process giving research communities back control of publishing | Insights
content: Abstract: The current academic publishing system faces many well-identified issues. Not only is it slow and costly, but it is also an opaque system that produces a substantial amount of non-reproducible results. Peer Community In (PCI) is a non-profit organization that allows research communities to organize the open and free peer review of preprints on different thematic platforms. The authors of preprints that are recommended by these platforms can then choose to submit them to any journal or to Peer Community Journal (PCJ), a diamond open access journal, which publishes any and only PCI-recommended preprints. Because PCI follows the highest standards for evaluations and openness, many institutions and journals publicly recognize PCI-recommended preprints as being of similar value to accepted journal articles. This two-step process hence decouples the evaluation of research articles from their publication, while offering a free open access publication venue for its recommended preprints. Doing so allows researchers to reappropriate the publishing system, and the increasing number of submissions, publications and communities shows a growing demand for such alternative publishing

title: Open Here, There, and Everywhere? A Comparative Review of Divergences in Open Access Typologies and Definitions in Monitoring Instruments - Archive ouverte HAL
content: Louis Bachaud, Didier Torny. Open Here, There, and Everywhere? A Comparative Review of Divergences in Open Access Typologies and Definitions in Monitoring Instruments. 2026. ⟨hal-05661835⟩ Open Access (OA) monitoring has become a policy instrument for governments, institutions, and research funders globally, yet the typologies and definitions underlying OA monitors have never been systematically compared. Drawing on a global dataset of 30 OA monitors, we conduct the first systematic comparative review of their operational OA definitions and typologies. Our findings reveal dynamics of standardization, as well as enduring differences. Unpaywall/OpenAlex occupies a near-hegemonic position as metadata provider (77% of monitors), shaping classification choices across the landscape. Nevertheless, significant heterogeneity persists: monitors diverge on whether Bronze publications constitute true OA, on how to count Green OA publications, on the relevance of open licenses, and on whether economic dimensions (APCs, Diamond OA) belong in typologies at all. Only two monitors share both a common typology and identical operational criteria. These divergences pose challenges for cross-national
snippet: Louis Bachaud, Didier Torny. Open Here, There, and Everywhere? A Comparative Review of Divergences in Open Access Typologies and Definitions in Monitoring Instruments. 2026. ⟨hal-05661835⟩ Open Access
sourceDescription:

title: Economic analysis of options for improving EU legislative and regulatory frameworks with impact on access and reuse of publicly funded R&I results and of publications and data for scientific purposes - Publications Office of the EU
content: "This study conducted a comprehensive economic analysis of the current practices, market economics and dynamics, as well as EU legislative and regulatory frameworks concerning the access and reuse of publicly funded research and innovation results, publications, and data. Building on that it assessed the economic impact of policy options regarding EU-wide secondary publication right and harmonisation of the copyright exception for research."

title: From Access to Reuse: What a New European Commission Study Means for Open Science
content:
"The Publications Office of the European Union has published the final report of the study Economic analysis of options for improving EU legislative and regulatory frameworks with impact on access and reuse of publicly funded R&I results and of publications and data for scientific purposes.

Europe has made real progress on Open Access. But being able to read a paper is not the same as being able to reuse it. Research reuse means being able to analyse scientific outputs, combine them with other sources, process them automatically, and build new knowledge and services on top of them. This is the gap at the centre of the new European Commission study: the move from access to reuse.

The report provides a detailed economic analysis of how legal frameworks, publishing models, research infrastructures, and technical conditions interact. It focuses on two main policy areas: an EU-wide Secondary Publication Right (SPR) and the harmonisation of the copyright exception for research. Together, these two areas are central to the future of access to and reuse of publicly funded research results, publications, and data in Europe."

title: Open Research Bulletin
content: "The Open Research Bulletin (ORB), launched in 2026 by the University of Sussex's Open Research Group, aims to be a dynamic resource that addresses the evolving needs of researchers worldwide. It provides timely updates and context on the latest standards, policies, and best practices in open research. With a diverse readership, ORB is dedicated to supporting the global research community and those who champion open research. Each issue focuses on a distinct theme related to Open Research, and features a diverse collection of articles that explore key topics and offer practical guidance. These resources are designed to enhance researchers' understanding and fluency in open research principles, helping them navigate and adopt best practices with confidence. In our News & Events section, we spotlight recent awards and recognitions given to individuals who are advancing the awareness and implementation of open research practices. We also invite readers from around the globe to share announcements about recent accolades or upcoming open research-related events by emailing us at open.research.bulletin@sussex.ac.uk."

title: Launch of new online publication dedicated to providing updates and insights on the practicalities of open research | UK Reproducibility Network
content:
"Announcing the publication of the first issue of the Open Research Bulletin!

Founded by the Open Research Group at the University of Sussex and published by Open Press University of Sussex (OPUS), the ORB is dedicated to supporting the global research community and those who champion open research. This dynamic resource provides researchers and practitioners worldwide with updates on the latest debates, policies, and best practices in open research.

Each issue focuses on a distinct theme related to open research, featuring a diverse collection of articles that explore key topics and offer practical guidance, written by experts from across the open research community."

title: Goethe-Universität — Welcome to Goethe University Frankfurt
content:
"Frankfurt University Library’s Open Access publication fund offers financial support to researchers publishing in high-quality, peer-reviewed Open Access journals. The renewed DFG funding secures the continuation of this support and represents an important component of Open Access financing at Goethe University, particularly at a time of reduced public funding....

An important change will take effect on July 1, 2026: the CC BY (Attribution) license will become a mandatory requirement for funding through the publication fund. Developed by the organization Creative Commons, CC BY is widely regarded as the international standard for open scientific publishing. The license allows academic work to be shared, distributed, and adapted for both commercial and non-commercial purposes, provided the original author is properly credited. This level of openness significantly expands the reach and impact of academic publications while also providing a clear legal framework for reuse."

title: FRELIP — Open Access Discovery, born in Nigeria
content:
"We Provide Free Access to Open Knowledge & Digital Learning Resources

Our mission is to make quality education accessible to learners across Africa and the world by providing a centralized platform for Open Access journals, books, datasets, and research materials. We combine modern digital tools with curated academic resources to support students, educators, and researchers at all levels.

Through this platform, users can discover, explore, and engage with trusted academic content

We are committed to empowering education with reliable information, accessible learning, and technology-driven research support."

title: Open Science Portal
content: "The UNESCO Open Science Platform is your open gateway to the knowledge produced by the UNESCO Natural Sciences Family. It provides open access to original publications, including scientific articles, desk studies, reports and policy-oriented papers, produced or supported by UNESCO. The platform also provides practical insights from these studies through highlights, summaries and dashboards."

title: Launch of UNESCO Open Science Platform | UNESCO
content:
"UNESCO invites partners, researchers and the wider public to discover a new digital platform that promotes open access to UNESCO scientific knowledge and research products and tracks global progress on open science....

UNESCO is pleased to announce the public launch of the UNESCO Open Science Platform during the UN Open Source Week 2026, from 22 to 26 June 2026 in New York. Developed in partnership with the United Nations International Computing Centre (UNICC), the platform is released as free and open-source software and is built on open technologies, including a customized implementation of CERN's InvenioRDM, an open source framework for building large-scale digital academic repositories and platforms, as a core component. With an initial focus on research produced or supported by UNESCO's Natural Sciences Family, the platform aims to promote UNESCO-related knowledge and support the implementation of the UNESCO Recommendation on Open Science."

title: Multilingual Search Pilot | LA Referencia
content: "Scholarly knowledge is published in many languages. Yet relevant work can remain invisible when discovery depends on exact words. This guided pilot compares keyword, semantic and hybrid retrieval over a LA Referencia subset."

title: Connecting the Opens (VI): Senior leadership advancing openness in Higher Education | Zenodo
content:
"This item is the sixth and final in a series exploring how university leaders across Europe are connecting open science and open education. Based on interviews conducted by SPARC Europe, the series aims to inspire more leaders to connect the opens and support institutions in addressing complex challenges through an integrated open knowledge framework.

This item explores how senior leaders translate advocacy into strategic action to embed openness as a core institutional value. It highlights how leadership shapes governance, secures budgets for capacity building, and coordinates efforts across libraries, IT, and research support. Finally, it examines how leaders drive cultural change by rewarding open practices in career progression, supporting grassroots communities, and positioning their institutions within global open ecosystems."

title: Data sharing must evolve towards data sovereignty | The BMJ
content: "For more than a decade, data sharing has been championed as a cornerstone of scientific progress. Journals, funders, and global health bodies have pushed for transparency and reuse of datasets to enable new research questions to be asked, findings to be validated, and discoveries to be accelerated. These ambitions were right and necessary, but data sharing as it currently exists has not fully delivered on its promise. In an era of constrained resources and growing global health threats, this model needs to move beyond centralised control of data towards equitable, distributed analysis for fairer and faster evidence generation."

title: GitHub and Google Colab for Sharing Data and Code in: Journal of Insurance Medicine Volume 53: Issue 2 | Journal of Insurance Medicine
content: "A traditional print journal is not ideal for the dissemination of computer code and related data. Without the actual code and data, reproducibility cannot be assessed, and there can be no validation of the premise of such an article. Fortunately, there are inexpensive modern ways to share both data and code while providing for explanatory text: GitHub and Google Colaboratory. This article introduces both and provides 3 different examples for the reader to experience."

title: Publishing Without Borders: Advancing Equity in Scientific Authorship | Cureus
content: Abstract: Researchers from low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) remain underrepresented in global academic publishing despite their substantial contributions to science and public health. Evidence indicates that only a small proportion of articles in leading medical journals originate from LMIC regions that account for the majority of the global population, alongside limited representation in editorial leadership, which remains largely concentrated in high-income countries (HICs). Structural factors, including constrained access to mentorship, limited editorial inclusion, and persistent gender and geographic disparities, continue to restrict equitable participation. These challenges may also influence publication outcomes, contributing to lower acceptance rates, higher desk rejections, and reduced visibility of manuscripts from LMIC authors compared with those from HICs. Although mentorship and capacity-building initiatives have been introduced to address these gaps, their impact remains limited, as many programs operate at a small scale, engage relatively few participants, and lack long-term follow-up for sustained improvements in publication outcomes. title: US's climate.gov site, taken down by Trump, relaunched by nonprofit
content:
Over decades, researchers in the US government and programs it sponsored built up a tremendous number of climate resources, from comprehensive analyses to massive datasets to basic explainers meant to inform the public. And people within the government built the climate.gov website to make it all accessible. But if you try to navigate there today, you get redirected to the climate page of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, and are greeted with the following message:

In compliance with Executive Order 14303 (“Restoring Gold Standard Science”), the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy’s June 23, 2025 Memorandum (“Agency Guidance for Implementing Gold Standard Science in the Conduct & Management of Scientific Activities”), 15 USC § 2904 (“National Climate Program”), 15 USC § 2934 (“National Global Change Research Plan”), and 33 USC § 893a (“NOAA Ocean and Atmospheric Science Education Programs”), you have been redirected to NOAA.gov. Future research products previously housed under Climate.gov will be available at NOAA.gov/climate and its affiliate websites.

title: New UG Open Access Regulations | Open Science | Rijksuniversiteit Groningen
content: On 6 May 2026, the Board of Deans approved new Open Access Regulations, which will take effect on 1 September 2026. Building on the university’s strong open access record, the regulations mark a shift towards fostering a more diverse, sustainable and community-driven scholarly publishing system. They draw on an earlier policy implemented at the Faculty of Behavioural and Social Sciences, as well as on the new national Open Access Strategy.

title: Championing Public Data Data Professionals in Civic Life
content:
"The theme of this conference — Championing Data: Data Professionals at the Nexus — reflects the difficult reality we are in. But it also points to a belief that although we may be up against many challenges (political, financial, technological, and more), we are facing that future together. And that matters.

In this talk, I want to reflect on the role we play in academia and in the wider world. I want to think with you about who we are, what we have as a data community, and what we’re being called to do next. My argument is that our work as data professionals is civic work, even when we describe it as service or support. The events of the past year have made that civic role impossible to ignore."

title: OASPA 2026 Programme - OASPA
content:
"The OASPA 2026 annual conference will take place at Hotel Dubrovnik, Zagreb in Croatia from 21 – 23 September 2026. 

OASPA’s annual conference is the only event that brings together the whole of the open access community, accelerating connection, discovery and new thinking.

Join us this September in Croatia to exchange thoughts with a global network of experts and peers, and to help us shape the future of open access.

The programme will include keynote talks, panel discussions, poster lightning talk sessions, an interactive workshop session as well as plenty of time for networking during breaks and receptions."

title: AAC&U Research Identifies Open Educational Resources as a New High-Impact Practice
content:
"The American Association of Colleges and Universities (AAC&U) today released Open Educational Resources: A New High-Impact Practice, a groundbreaking national research report demonstrating that open educational resources (OER) can positively influence student success outcomes while making higher education more accessible and affordable.

Drawing on nearly 700,000 student records from 15 colleges and universities representing six Carnegie-derived institutional classifications, as well as survey and focus group data from more than 200 instructors, the two-year study is among the largest and most comprehensive examinations of OER implementation conducted to date."

title: Poster, Presentation, Protocol or Paper | We Never Thought We’d Need to Do This: Locating and Preserving At-Risk Federal Publications in Hyrax | ID: 6d570d000 | Carolina Digital Repository
content:
Abstract:  In February 2025, many datasets were removed from United States federal government websites. This alarming development led library staff at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill (UNC-CH) to worry that federally funded articles and research outputs might follow. To preserve UNC-CH authored research in service of UNC-CH's Open Access Policy, librarians rapidly developed and deployed the Federal Repository Harvesting Project. For this project, we identified UNC-CH authored research held in multiple federal repositories, harvested metadata and available PDFs, and uploaded the outputs to our Hyrax-based institutional repository. 

In this presentation, I will describe the project, including our repository identification criteria, our methods of harvesting eligible content, and the ingest process. I will reflect on the lessons that our team learned as we deployed a project rapidly to address a continually changing issue. Finally, I will address the changes that we have made to our repository collection strategy to convert the project from a one-off to an ongoing process.

title: Webinar: UKRI Open Access Policy Implementation (2026-2030)
content:
"Join us for a discussion with open access practitioners at research-performing organisations (RPOs) as we move into the next phase of implementing the UKRI Open Access Policy for research articles.

This session is an opportunity to come together to explore how organisations are approaching implementation in a changing open access landscape. Alongside updates from UKRI and perspectives from Jisc, the webinar will provide space for shared reflection, discussion, and exchange of experiences across the community.

Following UKRI’s recent communications and public announcement, we will provide further context and clarification on policy implementation and future direction. We also want to hear from participants about their experiences, questions, and practical considerations shaping implementation within their organisations."

title: Funding Open Access Articles – What Are the Options? - TIB AV-Portal
content: "In this video the financing behind Open Access publishing is unpacked, revealing why articles can be free to read while still incurring costs. The video outlines the primary mechanisms and offers a concise guide to the options researchers can explore before submitting a manuscript. This video was produced as part of the BMFTR-funded project open-access.network."

title: Measuring the Openness of Science: How? The Principles of Open Science Monitoring (webinar) - Open science monitoring initiative - OSMI
content: "As open science becomes a cornerstone of public policy, decision-makers and research stakeholders face a pivotal challenge: how do we actually measure success? Without a standardised way to track progress, accountability and long-term impact remain out of reach. Organised by OSMI and The Forum for Open Research in MENA (FORM), this session introduces the Principles of Open Science Monitoring and explores how they provide a structured approach to assessing the openness of research while remaining adaptable to unique regional contexts."

title: OSMI Working Group 3 is seeking case studies from scholarly content providers - Open science monitoring initiative - OSMI
content:
"The OSMI Working Group ‘Open science monitoring with scholarly content providers’ is seeking submissions of case studies from scholarly content providers (publishers, institutional, generalist, or specialist repositories, infrastructure providers, preprint servers, or other organizations that produce, aggregate or host scholarly content) showcasing the ways in which diverse scholarly content providers are implementing – or considering the implementation – of open science monitoring activities. We want to illustrate approaches that are emerging, to give other scholarly content providers pointers and inspiration to adopt open science monitoring practices. 

If you are a scholarly content provider willing to provide information on your approach to open science monitoring, please consider submitting your case study via this form."

title: TO REFORM SCIENTIFIC PUBLISHING, UNIVERSITIES MUST BE PUBLISHERS
content: Scientific publication faces growing problems. It is too expensive. Fake and predatory journals abound. Paper mills and peer review cartels corrupt. Journal-based peer review fails to prevent publication of poor and unreliable research. AI is generating completely fabricated data sets and full manuscripts. Peer review is being overwhelmed.
